learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Two Pointers, Loops, Peak Detection, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine tracking the altitude during a hike. A valid mountain requires climbing continuously to a highest point and then descending continuously. Your goal is to find the longest such hike.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine tracking the altitude during a hike. A valid mountain requires climbing continuously to a highest point and then descending continuously. Your goal is to find the longest such hike.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Peak Expansion pattern whenever a problem involves finding increasing-then-decreasing sequences around a peak.
learning.signals: Whenever the problem asks for an increasing sequence followed immediately by a decreasing sequence, think of Peak Expansion.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array arr. A mountain is defined as a subarray with length at least 3 where the values strictly increase to a peak and then strictly decrease. Return the length of the longest mountain. If there is no mountain, return 0.

examples.explanation: The longest mountain is [1,4,7,3,2] with length 5.
No valid mountain exists because there is no strictly increasing and decreasing sequence.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every subarray and verify whether it forms a mountain.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: A mountain always has a peak. For every valid peak, expand left while elements are increasing and expand right while elements are decreasing. The total expanded length is one mountain.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Every valid mountain has exactly one peak.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever the problem asks for an increasing sequence followed immediately by a decreasing sequence, think of Peak Expansion.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Every element is visited at most a constant number of times while expanding mountains, resulting in linear time complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever the problem asks for an increasing sequence followed immediately by a decreasing sequence, think of Peak Expansion.
commonWrongPaths: Ignoring the requirement of both uphill and downhill.
Accepting mountains of length less than 3.

hints: A mountain must have both an uphill and downhill.
Every peak satisfies arr[i-1] < arr[i] > arr[i+1].
commonMisconceptions: Ignoring the requirement of both uphill and downhill.
Accepting mountains of length less than 3.

pattern.name: Peak Expansion
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever the problem asks for an increasing sequence followed immediately by a decreasing sequence, think of Peak Expansion.

approaches.title: Check Every Subarray
approaches.idea: Generate every subarray and verify whether it forms a mountain.

code.java: Brute force omitted due to high complexity.

approaches.title: Dynamic Programming
approaches.idea: Compute increasing lengths from the left and decreasing lengths from the right. Combine them to determine mountain lengths.
algorithm: For every index, if both values are greater than zero, compute mountain length.

approaches.title: Peak Expansion
approaches.idea: Traverse the array to find every valid peak. From each peak, expand left while values are strictly increasing and expand right while values are strictly decreasing. Calculate the mountain length and skip processed elements.
approaches.intuition: Every element is visited at most a constant number of times while expanding mountains, resulting in linear time complexity.
algorithm: Check if current element is a peak.
Continue from the end of the current mountain.
approaches.dryRun:
arr=[2,1,4,7,3,2,5]
Peak=7
Expand Left→1
Expand Right→2
Mountain=[1,4,7,3,2]
Length=5
Answer=5

visualization.description: Find a peak, then expand to both sides.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using DP?
Why does every mountain need a peak?
How do duplicate values affect the answer?
Can the actual mountain be returned?
How would you count all mountains?
interviewTips: State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Every valid mountain has exactly one peak.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Find Peak → Expand Left → Expand Right → Update Maximum.

importantPoints: Peak must satisfy left < peak > right.
Mountain length must be at least 3.
Expansion visits each element only a few times.
commonMistakes: Ignoring the requirement of both uphill and downhill.
Accepting mountains of length less than 3.

relatedProblems: Peak Index in a Mountain Array
